children.name: Lycosa tarantula
children.description: The original tarantula -- name derives from Taranto in southern Italy. Historically blamed for tarantism. The New World theraphosids later borrowed the name.

children.name: Lycosa praegrandis
children.description: One of the largest European wolf spiders, found from the Balkans to the Middle East; digs deep silk-lined burrows.

children.name: Lycosa singoriensis
children.description: A large burrowing wolf spider of the steppes from Eastern Europe to Central Asia; its bite is medically minor.

children.name: Lycosa erythrognatha
children.description: Common urban wolf spider of southern Brazil; historically one of the most frequent causes of spider bites in the region before antivenom studies cleared it of serious danger.

children.name: Lycosa hispanica
children.description: A large burrowing wolf spider of the Iberian Peninsula. Builds turreted burrows in sandy soils and is one of the largest wolf spiders in Europe.

children.name: Lycosa fasciapilosa
children.description: A large striped wolf spider of Central Asia and Siberia. The specific name refers to the tufts of hair on its robust legs.

children.name: Pardosa amentata
children.description: Extremely abundant in Sweden; on shores of lakes, bogs, and wet meadows. Females carry egg sacs and guard spiderlings. Males perform leg-waving courtship.

children.name: Pardosa lugubris
children.description: Common woodland wolf spider across Europe including Sweden; active on forest floor in spring and early summer.

children.name: Pardosa palustris
children.description: Abundant in open grassland and dunes across the Palearctic; an important predator of small insects in agricultural fields.

children.name: Pardosa monticola
children.description: Found in short dry grassland and heath; one of the commonest wolf spiders of European calcareous habitats.

children.name: Pardosa pullata
children.description: A very common small dark wolf spider of damp meadows and bogs throughout northern Europe.

children.name: Pardosa prativaga
children.description: A very common Pardosa of damp grasslands across Europe and Britain. Males have dark, hairy legs and perform distinctive visual courtship displays.

children.name: Pardosa agrestis
children.description: A Palearctic wolf spider of open fields and agricultural land. Adapted to disturbed habitats and an important natural pest controller in cereal crops.

children.name: Pardosa hortensis
children.description: A Mediterranean wolf spider that extends across southern Europe and into Central Asia. Often found in gardens and vineyards.

children.name: Pardosa saltans
children.description: A jumping wolf spider species, formerly part of the lugubris complex. Males perform elaborate visual courtship dances with raised legs.

children.name: Pardosa riparia
children.description: A small dark wolf spider of riverbanks and gravelly lakeshores across Europe. Well-camouflaged against the pebbly substrate.

children.name: Pardosa nigriceps
children.description: A striking sexually-dimorphic wolf spider: males have black head regions and brightly banded legs used in courtship displays. Common in European grasslands.

children.name: Pardosa hyperborea
children.description: A Holarctic wolf spider of the Arctic tundra. Active for only a few weeks during the brief Arctic summer. A key tundra predator of springtails and small flies.

children.name: Pardosa groenlandica
children.description: The only wolf spider of Greenland, also found in Iceland and Arctic Canada. Inhabits fell-fields and tundra; completes its life cycle over two Arctic summers.

children.name: Schizocosa
children.description: A genus of medium-sized wolf spiders known for the striking courtship displays of males, who sport dark bristle brushes (ornamentation) on the forelegs used in visual and seismic signalling to females. Found across North America.

children.name: Schizocosa crassipalpis
children.description: A common North American Schizocosa, named for the male's swollen dark palps. Males have dense black bristles on the forelegs used in courtship displays.

children.name: Schizocosa ocreata
children.description: The best-studied Schizocosa. Males have black brushes on the fore tibiae and perform elaborate leg-waving courtship combining visual and vibratory signals. A model organism in spider behavioural evolution research.

children.name: Schizocosa rostrata
children.description: A North American Schizocosa with a distinctively projecting (beaked) carapace. Males lack the dense brushes of S. ocreata but still perform courtship displays.

children.name: Schizocosa avida
children.description: A large, aggressive wolf spider of the eastern and central US. Males have moderate leg brushes and use a combination of visual and seismic signals during courtship.

children.name: Schizocosa stridulans
children.description: A southeastern US Schizocosa that produces audible stridulatory sounds during courtship, in addition to visual displays.

children.name: Schizocosa ceratophila
children.description: A Schizocosa from the southeastern US with distinctive horn-like projections on the male palps. Closely related to S. stridulans.

children.name: Sosippus
children.description: Unique among wolf spiders for building a large, funnel-shaped web with a silken sheet, somewhat resembling a grass spider web. Unlike most lycosids, which are wandering hunters, Sosippus waits at the funnel entrance for prey.

children.name: Sosippus mimus
children.description: A Florida and Gulf Coast web-building wolf spider that constructs a funnel-web sheet in low vegetation. Specimens were formerly misidentified as other funnel-web species.

children.name: Sosippus floridanus
